mnemonics.title: Programmed I/O (PIO) Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: Programmer Initiates Operation – the CPU starts, controls, and completes the entire I/O transaction without any help from the device.

mnemonics.title: Interrupt‑driven I/O Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: Interrupt Request: Immediate Response Keeps CPU Efficient – the device signals the CPU, which suspends its current work, services the request, then resumes.

mnemonics.title: DMA Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: Direct Memory Access: Data Moves Automatically – the DMA controller transfers data directly between I/O device and memory, bypassing the CPU.

mnemonics.title: ISR Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: Interrupt Service Routine: I Serve, Return – the small piece of code that runs when an interrupt occurs, serves the device, then returns control to the interrupted task.

acronyms.usage: CPU continuously polls the I/O device's status register to check if data is ready; simplest I/O method, no extra hardware.
A hardware line that a device toggles to tell the CPU it needs attention; triggers interrupt handling.
The function executed in response to an interrupt; handles the device’s request and restores CPU state.
A hardware module that transfers data between memory and I/O devices autonomously, freeing CPU for other tasks.
Signal sent by a peripheral to the DMA controller to request a data transfer; usually accompanied by address and transfer size.
Register in I/O devices used to control device operation (start, stop) and to read its current status (busy, done, error).

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a teacher (CPU) handing a worksheet to each student (I/O device) one by one, waiting for each to finish before moving to the next.
Picture a receptionist (CPU) who lets you work (main program) but only stops you when a phone rings (interrupt), then handles the call and resumes work.

Think of a dedicated courier van (DMA controller) that picks up packages (data) from the sender (device) and drops them directly at the recipient's doorstep (memory) without the owner (CPU) having to carry them.

analogies.analogy: Writing a letter by hand: you (CPU) write each word, check the paper, and then mail it yourself; no assistance from anyone else.
A kitchen timer that beeps when done, prompting you to stop cooking and check the food; you resume cooking after handling the beep.
Hiring a moving company to transport furniture directly from the old house to the new one while you stay elsewhere; you don’t have to carry each piece yourself.
